title: Estimating Bad Debts — % of Sales vs Aging
event: Ortiz Co. estimates uncollectibles at year-end. Credit sales were $600,000, and the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts has an existing credit balance of $2,000. Prepare the adjusting entry.

lines.label: 600,000 × 1%
lines.why: The sales method matches expense to the revenue that created the risk — the existing allowance balance is IGNORED.
lines.trap: Don't net the 2,000 here — %-of-sales computes the EXPENSE directly, not an ending balance.

lines.label: cushion grows
lines.why: The contra-asset absorbs future write-offs; ending balance becomes 8,000.
lines.trap: Never credit AR itself — no specific customer has defaulted yet.

lines.label: 9,000 target − 2,000 existing
lines.why: Aging computes the required ENDING allowance; the entry is whatever plugs the gap.
lines.trap: The existing balance matters HERE and not in %-of-sales — the single most-tested contrast in the chapter.

lines.label: topped up to 9,000
lines.why: Allowance rises from 2,000 to exactly the 9,000 the aging demands.
lines.trap: If the existing balance were a DEBIT 2,000, the entry would be 11,000 — direction of the existing balance matters.

memorize.body: % of sales → expense DIRECTLY (ignore existing allowance). Aging → ending BALANCE (net the existing allowance).
Net realizable value = gross AR − allowance. Only the aging method targets NRV explicitly.

questions.prompt: Aging requires a $9,000 ending allowance; the account holds a $2,000 credit. Bad debt expense = ?

distractors.feedback: That's the required ENDING balance. Part of it already exists — the entry adds only the 7,000 gap.
You treated the existing 2,000 as a DEBIT balance. It's a credit — subtract it: 9,000 − 2,000.

build.scaffold: First question: which method? Sales % → compute expense directly. Aging → target minus existing balance.
